[180:01]It was part of a series of three amendments to the Constitution

[180:13]of the freed slaves after the conclusion of the Civil War.

[180:19]The 13th Amendment ended slavery.

[180:21]The 14th Amendment,

[180:23]which is where birthright citizenship is enshrined,

[180:26]was ratified so that the people that were freed

[180:30]from slavery would be grandfathered in as citizens.

[182:07]citizenship.

[182:08]But you have to understand that in order to do this, there's a few ways you could go

[182:12]about it.

[182:14]Since it is enshrined in the Constitution and was interpreted by the Supreme Court in

[182:18]the late 19th century, there are one of three ways that we can go about getting rid of this.

[182:25]you can pass a law in Congress, which says people born on the land are no longer automatically citizens.

[182:33]So there's a federal statute. You can pass a law in Congress.

[182:37]The obvious way to go about it is you can amend the Constitution.

[182:41]You either have a convention of states or it's a supermajority in Congress.

[182:46]There's two ways to ratify a new amendment in the Constitution.

[182:57]That's another way.

[182:59]And then the third way is you could catalyze a Supreme Court decision on that provision

[183:06]in the 14th Amendment.

[183:07]You can either have a federal law or you could have, in this case, an executive order, force

[183:12]the Supreme Court to consider this issue.

[183:16]And then it would give the Supreme Court an opportunity to create a new interpretation

[183:20]of the 14th Amendment and maybe redefine it in a different way.

[183:24]Those are the three ways you might go about it.

[183:27]Obviously, amending the constitution is the most rock solid, but also the most difficult.
